section.title: A Warning Against Adultery
section.sectionRationale: The fifth discourse gives the book's first sustained, single subject warning: the sweet but deadly words of the forbidden woman, verses 1 to 6, the ruin of the one who is seduced, verses 7 to 14, and, in deliberate contrast, the God given joy of faithful marriage, verses 15 to 20, closing with the LORD's all seeing eye over every path, verses 21 to 23.
section.thesis: The path to the forbidden woman looks sweet but ends in bitterness and ruin, while the path of faithful, joyful marriage is God's own good gift; both paths lie open before the eyes of the LORD, who examines every step.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the deceptive sweetness of the forbidden woman, verses 1 to 6; the devastating regret of the one who is seduced, verses 7 to 14; the call to rejoice instead in the wife of one's youth, verses 15 to 20; and the LORD's all seeing gaze over every path, verses 21 to 23.

units.label: The deceptive sweetness of the forbidden woman

verses.text: My son, pay attention to my wisdom; incline your ear to my insight,
that you may maintain discretion and your lips may preserve knowledge.
Though the lips of the {{zarah|forbidden woman}} drip honey and her speech is smoother than oil,
in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a double-edged sword.
Her feet go down to death; her steps lead straight to Sheol.
She does not consider the path of life; she does not know that her ways are unstable.

units.label: The regret of the one who is seduced

verses.text: So now, my sons, listen to me, and do not turn aside from the words of my mouth.
Keep your path far from her; do not go near the door of her house,
lest you concede your vigor to others, and your years to one who is cruel;
lest strangers feast on your wealth, and your labors enrich the house of a foreigner.
At the end of your life you will groan when your flesh and your body are spent,
and you will say, "How I hated {{musar|discipline}}, and my heart despised reproof!
I did not listen to the voice of my teachers or incline my ear to my mentors.
I am on the brink of utter ruin in the midst of the whole assembly."

units.label: Rejoice in the wife of your youth

verses.text: Drink water from your own cistern, and running water from your own well.
Why should your springs flow in the streets, your streams of water in the public squares?
Let them be yours alone, never to be shared with strangers.
May your fountain be blessed, and may you rejoice in the wife of your youth:
A loving doe, a graceful fawn, may her breasts satisfy you always; may you be {{shagah|captivated}} by her love forever.
Why be {{shagah|captivated}}, my son, by an adulteress, or embrace the bosom of a stranger?
For a man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD examines all his paths.
The iniquities of a wicked man entrap him; the cords of his sin entangle him.
He dies for lack of discipline, led astray by his own great folly.

zarah.gloss: The forbidden or strange woman, one who lures with smooth, honeyed speech that hides a bitter and deadly end. She appears repeatedly across chapters 2 through 7, always with the same pattern, sweet at first taste, ruinous in the end.

musar.gloss: Discipline or correction. The seduced man's greatest regret, voiced in verse 12, is not the pleasure he pursued but the discipline he despised and the teachers he refused to hear, showing that this ruin was preventable all along.

shagah.gloss: To be captivated, intoxicated, or led astray, the same root used for both proper and improper devotion in this passage. Verse 19 commands being captivated by one's own wife; verse 20 asks why a son would let himself be captivated, the identical word, by a woman not his own.

basic.context: This chapter is the book's first extended, single subject warning, and its subject is adultery. It paints the forbidden woman's words as honey that turns to bitter poison, pictures in devastating detail the regret of a man who ignored every warning, and then, in deliberate contrast, calls a husband to find deep and lasting joy in his own wife. The chapter ends by reminding the reader that the LORD sees every path, so no choice here is ever truly hidden.
basic.hermeneutics: This is direct moral instruction addressed to a young man, using vivid, sensory language, honey, oil, wormwood, a sword, to make an abstract danger feel immediate and real. Its warning is not against marriage or sexual desire, both are affirmed and even celebrated in verses 15 to 19, but against unfaithfulness, which the passage treats as a path that destroys the very things, vigor, wealth, reputation, and life itself, that it falsely promises to enrich.
basic.originalLanguages: The zarah, forbidden woman, is described with a taste metaphor, honey turning to wormwood, showing that her appeal is real but temporary and reversing. The son's deepest regret in verse 12 is hating musar, discipline, not merely the consequences of sin. And the same Hebrew root, shagah, describes being captivated both rightly, by one's own wife, verse 19, and wrongly, by an adulteress, verse 20, showing that the issue is not desire itself but its rightly or wrongly placed object.
basic.typology: The picture of a path that looks sweet but leads down to death and Sheol, verses 4 to 5, anticipates the New Testament's warning that sin, when it is full grown, brings forth death, James 1:15. The call to find joy exclusively in the wife of one's youth, verses 15 to 19, foreshadows the deeper union and exclusive devotion Paul later applies to Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32.
basic.christ: This chapter's warning exposes a debt every human heart owes, since all have been captivated by wrong loves at some point, whether sexual or otherwise, and all deserve the entrapment described in verse 22. The gospel meets this exact problem: Christ was cut off, entangled in the cords of sin though He committed none, so that those bound by their own iniquities could be set free, and He calls His people, the bride He loves exclusively and eternally, to the same faithful, joyful devotion this chapter commends within marriage.
basic.god: God is the giver of marital joy, blessing the fountain and the wife of one's youth, verse 18, not merely tolerating desire but designing it for exclusive delight. He is also the all seeing judge, verse 21, before whom no secret path is ever truly hidden, and under whose moral order sin's own cords become the very trap that catches the sinner.
basic.crossReferences: James 1:15 traces the trajectory of sin to death, echoing verses 4 to 5. Hebrews 13:4 calls marriage to be held in honor and the marriage bed kept undefiled. Ephesians 5:31 to 32 applies exclusive marital union to Christ and the church. Galatians 6:7 to 8 states the same principle as verse 22, sowing to the flesh reaps corruption.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's warning against a sweet path that ends in death, and its celebration of joyful faithfulness instead, sits within Scripture's larger story of humanity's wandering hearts and God's persistent covenant love. That story reaches its center in Christ, who bore the entangling cords of sin for His unfaithful people and now calls them, His bride, to the same exclusive, joyful devotion this chapter commends between husband and wife.

context.literary: A tightly structured warning discourse, moving from description of the danger, verses 1 to 6, to its devastating personal cost, verses 7 to 14, to a positive alternative, verses 15 to 20, and closing with a theological grounding in God's all seeing justice, verses 21 to 23, a pattern of warning followed by a better way that recurs across chapters 5 to 7.
context.historical: Ancient Israelite wisdom instruction took seriously the social and economic devastation adultery could cause, loss of wealth to strangers, verse 10, public shame, verse 14, and even legal or violent retribution, developed further in chapter 6, making this warning as practical and social as it is moral.
context.canonical: This chapter begins a sustained theme across chapters 5 to 7 warning against the forbidden woman, a theme that also serves, alongside the personification of Wisdom herself, to frame two competing feminine voices calling for the young man's heart throughout the book's opening discourses.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn vividly against the false promise of adultery by exposing both its deceptive sweetness and its devastating end, while commending the true and lasting joy God intends within faithful marriage, so that the reader chooses the second path before the first one closes behind him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands, keep your path far from her, rejoice in the wife of your youth, are directly prescriptive. The description of the seduced man's regret, verses 9 to 14, functions as a typical, cautionary portrait of where this path generally leads rather than a guarantee that every act of adultery results in identical, visible ruin in this life; the passage's real force is theological and moral, not a mechanical prediction of outcomes.

debates.question: Is this chapter's warning relevant only to men, since it addresses 'my son'?

views.label: Addressed specifically to young men in a patriarchal setting
views.case: The passage's direct address and its picture of the tempting woman reflect the book's consistent father to son form of address throughout chapters 1 to 9.

views.label: A principle that applies to both sexes
views.case: The underlying moral claims, that sexual sin promises sweetness and delivers ruin, that faithful marriage is God's good gift, and that the LORD sees every path, apply without remainder to women as much as men, even though the literary form addresses a son.

debates.bearing: The specific literary address to a son reflects its ancient setting, but its moral substance, guard against sexual temptation and treasure faithful marriage, binds all people, and the New Testament applies the same standard of faithfulness to both husbands and wives alike, 1 Corinthians 7:2-5.

study.originalLanguages: The zarah, forbidden woman, is described through taste, honey turning to wormwood, a vivid image of pleasure that reverses into bitterness. The son's regret in verse 12 centers on hating musar, discipline, showing the tragedy was avoidable instruction refused, not fate. And the verb shagah, captivated or led astray, is used of both the rightly ordered joy of one's own marriage, verse 19, and the wrongly placed devotion to an adulteress, verse 20, showing the passage's real concern is the object of devotion, not the existence of desire itself.

typology.type: A path that ends in death and Sheol, Proverbs 5:4-5
typology.fulfillment: James' teaching that sin, full grown, brings forth death, James 1:15, and the deliverance from that death offered in Christ.

typology.type: Exclusive joy in the wife of one's youth, Proverbs 5:15-19
typology.fulfillment: Christ's exclusive, covenantal love for His church, pictured as a marriage, Ephesians 5:31-32.

study.christ: This chapter names a universal danger, hearts captivated by the wrong love, and a universal remedy pattern, faithful, exclusive devotion. Every reader has, in some measure, tasted the honey that turns to wormwood, wandering from the path of life the chapter commends. The gospel meets this failure directly: Christ, though faithful in every way, was entangled in the cords of sin's judgment on behalf of His people, cut off in death so that those caught by their own iniquities, verse 22, could be released. And He calls His church, His bride, to the same undivided, joyful faithfulness this chapter commends to a husband and wife, Ephesians 5:31-32, Revelation 19:7.
study.god: God is the giver and blesser of marital joy, not a killjoy toward desire but its rightful designer, and He is also the LORD whose eyes examine every path, verse 21, so that even the most secret unfaithfulness is never truly hidden from Him.

crossReferences.note: Sin, when it is full grown, brings forth death, tracing the same trajectory as the forbidden woman's path in verses 4-5.
Marriage is to be held in honor by all, and the marriage bed kept undefiled, affirming the positive vision of verses 15-19.
Marriage as a picture of Christ's exclusive union with His church, echoing the chapter's call to joy in one spouse alone.
Whatever a man sows, he will also reap, echoing the entangling cords of sin in verse 22.

study.oneStory: This chapter warns that sin's sweetness always turns bitter and calls readers instead to the lasting joy of faithful love, while reminding them that the LORD sees every step. That warning and that invitation both point forward to Christ, who bore the entangling judgment of unfaithfulness for His people and now calls His bride to the same undivided, joyful devotion this chapter pictures in marriage.

sermon.title: Warning against Adultery
sermon.why: Begg walks directly through Proverbs 5, the exact passage of this study, warning of the consequences of infidelity and pointing to the joy of marital faithfulness as God's ordained gift, matching the chapter's own structure and aim.

quiz.q: How does the chapter describe the forbidden woman's appeal and its true end, verses 3 to 5?

options.text: Her words are sweet as honey at first, but in the end she is bitter as wormwood and leads to death.
options.note: This is the exact contrast the passage draws.

options.text: She offers no real appeal at all, only obvious danger.
options.note: The passage stresses her real, deceptive sweetness, not obvious repulsiveness.

options.text: She is dangerous only because of social gossip.
options.note: The danger described is far deeper, spiritual and mortal, not merely reputational.

options.text: Her appeal fades quickly but carries no lasting consequence.
options.note: The passage's point is the opposite, the appeal reverses into bitterness with lasting, even fatal, consequence.

quiz.q: What does the seduced man regret most, according to verses 12 to 13?

options.text: That he hated discipline and refused to listen to his teachers.
options.note: This is stated directly as his central regret, not merely the outward consequences.

options.text: That he was never warned at all.
options.note: The passage shows he was taught and warned but refused to listen.

options.text: That his family was too strict with him.
options.note: The passage places responsibility on his own refusal to listen, not on excessive parental strictness.

options.text: That he lacked the resources to succeed in life.
options.note: Financial loss is mentioned, verse 10, but his own stated regret centers on despising discipline, not resources.

quiz.q: What positive picture does the chapter give in verses 15 to 19?

options.text: Joyful, exclusive delight in the wife of one's youth, pictured as drinking from one's own well.
options.note: This is the chapter's positive alternative to the forbidden woman.

options.text: A command to avoid marriage altogether.
options.note: The passage celebrates marriage; it does not discourage it.

options.text: Permission to share intimacy widely as long as it is not called adultery.
options.note: The passage explicitly commands exclusivity, let them be yours alone, the opposite of sharing widely.

options.text: A picture of duty without any real joy.
options.note: The passage's language, rejoice, captivated, satisfy, is emotionally rich, not merely dutiful.

quiz.q: What does verse 21 say about the LORD's awareness of human conduct?

options.text: A man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD, who examines all his paths.
options.note: This is the exact statement, grounding the whole warning in God's all seeing justice.

options.text: God is unaware of private choices made in secret.
options.note: The verse states the opposite, nothing is hidden from Him.

options.text: God only judges public actions, not private ones.
options.note: The verse specifically emphasizes that all paths, including private ones, are examined by God.

options.text: God's awareness applies only to Israel's leaders.
options.note: The statement is general, about any man's ways, not limited to leaders.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's warning connect to the gospel?

options.text: It exposes a universal danger of misplaced devotion that Christ answers by bearing sin's entangling judgment and calling His people to faithful love.
options.note: This connects the chapter's warning and positive vision to Christ's atoning work and His relationship to the church, Ephesians 5:31-32.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the gospel.
options.note: The chapter's themes of sin, judgment, and faithful covenant love connect directly to the gospel's own categories.

options.text: It teaches that only sexual sins matter to God.
options.note: This chapter's specific focus is sexual faithfulness, but Scripture treats it as one of many entangling sins, not the sole concern of God.

options.text: It shows marriage is unnecessary once someone is in Christ.
options.note: The New Testament instead honors marriage highly, even using it as a picture of Christ and the church, rather than treating it as unnecessary.

questions: The forbidden woman's words are sweet at first and bitter in the end. Where in your own life have you seen a tempting shortcut promise pleasure and deliver regret instead?
The seduced man's deepest regret was hating discipline and ignoring his teachers. Is there current correction or counsel you are resisting that you may deeply regret ignoring later?

Verses 15 to 19 celebrate joyful, exclusive delight in marriage as God's good gift. If you are married, how are you actively cultivating that joy rather than taking it for granted or looking elsewhere?
Verse 21 says the LORD examines every path, even the hidden ones. How does knowing that God sees everything change what you do when no one else is watching?

Christ bore the entangling judgment of sin so unfaithful people could be set free and calls His church to faithful, joyful devotion. Where do you need His grace today for a wandering heart, whether in marriage or in some other area of misplaced desire?

translationNotes.note: Forbidden woman can also be rendered the adulteress; smoother than oil, or her palate, pictures speech so pleasant it seems to promise nothing but good.

May you be captivated renders a Hebrew verb that can also mean be led astray; the same root appears in verse 20 for wrongful captivation, forming a deliberate wordplay between rightly and wrongly placed devotion.
Stranger here, as in verse 10, can also be rendered a foreign woman or another man's wife, reinforcing that the danger is a woman outside the bond of one's own marriage covenant.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 5:1-23. One em dash present in the source at verse 19 was rewritten using a comma, and its poetic line break was rendered as flowing prose, to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
